mapQuestions.question: Which continent is South Korea on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the east. South Korea sits on the southern half of the Korean Peninsula, with North Korea to the north and the sea on three sides 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of South Korea?
mapQuestions.answer: Seoul, in the north-west, about 50 km from the North Korean border. Around half of all Koreans (25 million) live in the wider Seoul metro area.

mapQuestions.question: What language does South Korea speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Korean, written in a unique alphabet called "Hangul" invented in the 1400s. Hangul is so well designed that linguists call it one of the best writing systems ever made.

mapQuestions.question: Why are there TWO Koreas?
mapQuestions.answer: After Japan was defeated in WWII in 1945, the peninsula was split between Soviet and American zones along the 38th parallel. The two halves never reunited, and a heavily-guarded border (the DMZ) still divides them today.

mapQuestions.question: How mountainous is South Korea?
mapQuestions.answer: About 70% of the country is mountains. That's why South Korea's cities are so densely packed onto the small flat areas, and why the rest is hiking trails and national parks.

foods.name: Kimchi
foods.description: Spicy fermented cabbage, eaten at every single Korean meal as a side dish. Each Korean family has its own recipe and ferments huge batches of kimchi in autumn for the year ahead.

foods.name: Korean BBQ (gogi-gui)
foods.description: Marinated meat (bulgogi beef, samgyeopsal pork belly) grilled at your own table on a centre grill. You wrap the cooked meat in lettuce with rice, kimchi and garlic.

foods.name: Bibimbap
foods.description: "Mixed rice": a bowl of warm rice topped with sautéed vegetables, a fried egg, beef, and a dollop of red gochujang chilli paste. Mix the whole thing together before eating.

foods.name: Tteokbokki
foods.description: Chewy cylindrical rice cakes simmered in a sweet-spicy red gochujang sauce, with fishcake and boiled egg. The classic Korean street snack, sold from carts everywhere.

foods.name: Kimbap
foods.description: Korean rice rolls (similar to sushi but with seasoned rice and various fillings like pickled radish, beef, spinach, egg). The Korean picnic and packed-lunch classic.

foods.name: Bingsu
foods.description: Mountain-sized shaved-ice dessert topped with sweet red beans, fresh fruit, condensed milk, mochi pieces or matcha powder. Korea's summer favourite.

scavengerItems.label: A traditional palace (Gyeongbokgung in Seoul, Changdeokgung) with sweeping curved roofs
People wearing colourful "hanbok" (traditional silk robes) at a palace
Cherry blossoms in spring (April), or red maples in autumn (October)
A bullet-train KTX speeding between cities
A K-pop poster, billboard, or photocard shop
A street vendor selling tteokbokki, hotteok or grilled skewers
A mountain Buddhist temple with painted eaves and stone lanterns
EVERYONE using their phone on the subway (Korea has the fastest internet on Earth)
A neon-lit shopping street in Myeong-dong, Hongdae or Gangnam
A "jjimjilbang" (public bathhouse) with steaming pools and hot stone rooms
The "dol hareubang" stone grandfathers on Jeju Island
The "haenyeo" (women free-divers) of Jeju who dive for shellfish without scuba gear

geoMatches.description: The capital and biggest city. Modern skyscrapers right next to 600-year-old palaces, neon-lit streets, the Han River cutting through the middle, and one of the world's best subway systems.
The big southern port city. Beaches, fish markets, mountains right behind the coast, and the start point of many sea routes.
A volcanic island south of the peninsula. Hallasan (the tallest mountain in South Korea), tangerine groves, lava-tube caves, beach resorts, and the haenyeo women divers.
The capital of the ancient Silla kingdom (1,000+ years ago). Royal burial mounds, stone Buddhist temples, and Bulguksa, one of the most beautiful temples on the peninsula.
The Demilitarized Zone, the heavily-guarded 4-km strip dividing North and South Korea. Tours allow visitors to look across into North Korea from observatories.
The famous mountain national park in the north-east, with sharp granite peaks, ancient pine trees, and one of the most spectacular autumn-foliage seasons in Asia.

senses.smell: Garlic and sesame oil in Korean kitchens, fermenting kimchi pots, hot bulgogi smoke at a BBQ table, charcoal-roasted chestnuts on a Seoul street corner, the cold-mountain pine smell in Seoraksan…
senses.hear: K-pop EVERYWHERE (subway, taxis, shops), "Annyeonghaseyo!" greetings as you enter every shop, the woosh of high-speed trains, the hiss of meat hitting a hot grill, evening temple bells…
senses.taste: Spicy fermented kimchi, smoky grilled beef wrapped in lettuce, sweet red-bean bingsu shaved ice, salty-sweet tteokbokki rice cakes, hot Korean fried chicken with crackly skin…
senses.feel: Heated floors (ondol) under socks in Korean houses, hot bathhouse stones on your back, the steam of a kimchi-jjigae stew bowl on a cold day, the crowd of a Seoul subway at rush hour…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Hangul such a brilliant alphabet?
stories.body: For most of Korea's history, Koreans wrote in Chinese characters, which were extremely hard to learn. In 1443, King Sejong the Great decided to invent a brand new writing system designed specifically so that ordinary Koreans, including farmers and women (both of whom usually couldn't read), could become literate quickly. The result is "Hangul": 14 consonants and 10 vowels, with letter shapes that literally show how your mouth makes each sound. Hangul is so clever that some linguists call it the best-designed writing system ever invented. Modern Koreans can teach the basics of Hangul to a complete beginner in about an hour. King Sejong's face is on the 10,000-won banknote.

stories.question: What is "Hallyu" (the Korean Wave)?
stories.body: Around the year 2000, Korean TV dramas and pop music started becoming popular in China and Japan. By 2010 the wave had spread across Asia. By 2020 it was global: BTS, Blackpink, K-dramas on Netflix, the movie "Parasite" winning the Best Picture Oscar in 2020, the show "Squid Game" becoming the most-watched Netflix series ever. This whole movement is called "Hallyu" (한류), the Korean Wave. South Korean tourist numbers exploded as fans came to visit. K-pop alone is now worth several billion dollars a year. South Korea is the only country in the world to have gone from a major exporter of goods to a major exporter of CULTURE in just one generation.

stories.question: Who are the "haenyeo" women divers?
stories.body: On Jeju Island, off the southern coast, generations of women have made their living by FREE-diving (no scuba gear) into the cold sea to harvest shellfish, sea urchins, abalone and octopus. They are called "haenyeo" ("sea women"). They dive 10 to 20 metres down, hold their breath for over a minute, and surface with a distinctive whistling-breath called "sumbisori". Many haenyeo are over 70 years old, and some are over 90. UNESCO recognised the haenyeo as "intangible cultural heritage" in 2016. The tradition is fading though, because few young women want the cold, dangerous work. You can watch haenyeo come ashore at Jeju harbours in the late morning.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Some traditions stay alive only because individual older people keep doing them. What old skills in your family would you want to learn before they fade?

stories.question: Why is Korean food so spicy?
stories.body: Spicy red Korean food is iconic now, but it wasn't always like this. Chillies arrived in Korea only in the 1500s, brought from the Americas by Portuguese traders via Japan. Before then, Korean kimchi was white and salty, not red. Korean cooks took to chillies brilliantly. By the 1700s the bright-red gochugaru chilli powder and the fermented gochujang chilli paste had become central to Korean cooking, used in everything from kimchi to tteokbokki to stews. Most spicy Korean dishes are actually about 300 to 400 years old, not ancient. Korean fermentation traditions (the actual ancient part) make the chilli flavours rounder and deeper.

stories.question: What is the Demilitarized Zone?
stories.body: The 38th parallel was drawn across Korea in 1945 to split it into Soviet-aligned and American-aligned halves. Five years later, in 1950, North Korea invaded South Korea, sparking the Korean War, which lasted three brutal years. The war ended in 1953 with an ARMISTICE (a ceasefire), not a peace treaty, so technically the two Koreas are still at war today. The Demilitarized Zone, the DMZ, is a 4 km wide strip running 250 km right across the peninsula, with thousands of soldiers on each side. South Korea offers tours where visitors can look into North Korea from observation posts. The DMZ is, paradoxically, also one of the wildest places in Asia: 70 years of no humans means it is now an unintentional wildlife reserve, full of cranes, deer and rare plants.

convoQuestions.question: Have you tried saying "annyeonghaseyo" or "gamsahamnida" to anyone?
Big city Seoul, mountain temples, beach Busan, Jeju Island, which felt the most "Korea"?
Have you heard a K-pop song you really love this trip?
How does the speed of Korean cities compare to home?
King Sejong invented an entire alphabet so ordinary people could read. Would your country do something like that today?

wordSearch.hint: Korean foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Korean street cat
animals.description: In Seoul alleys, Jeju villages and temple grounds. Often fed by neighbours.

animals.name: Jindo dog
animals.description: A native Korean breed, famously loyal. Named after Jindo Island where they come from.

animals.name: Sika deer
animals.description: In Seoraksan National Park. The fawns are spotted; the bucks have branching antlers.

animals.name: Black kite
animals.description: Soaring over Seoul rivers and coastal cliffs.

animals.name: Magpie
animals.description: Black-and-white birds with iridescent blue wings. The unofficial national bird.

animals.name: Amur leopard
animals.description: Critically endangered, fewer than 100 left in the wild on the Korea-Russia-China border.

animals.name: Minke whale
animals.description: Off the east coast of Korea, especially around Ulsan and the Sea of Japan.
